speaker: interviewer
text: March 16, 1995. I am Donna Kanter. You were in Germany. Your brother had smuggled you to the eastern German border through Eastern Germany to Western Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and we, we were caught, like I said, by the-- by the Russians and ended up in a camp again. And they marched us for 72 kilometer to Hof. That was a big city on the eastern side of Germany, I think-- Hof. And where we had to go through kind of interrogation or whatever. But during that march, a lot of weird things happened because I really got along well with the Russians. I had no problem. The Russians, they carried me on their shoulders. They put me on, on all kinds of like-- it was wintertime. So they put me on a sleigh. They put me on a wagon at one point. And here I was wearing those wonderful boots from Auschwitz, those great boots that got heavier by the minute because-- first of all, because they were wet. They got wet because it was made out of felt. It wasn't leather. So they got wet. And they soaked through. But that was the only shoes I had. And I remember when, when the Russians put me on, on a-- on a bus-- not a bus, a truck. Somebody was driving by. He stopped them, you know. And they talk rough, those Russians. And I knew how to talk rough too. I was very rough. And there were two little cities. And they were like two kilometers away. I was two kilometers away from that little town where they were going to stop for the night, the Russians, because we marched 72 kilometers, which took us two days, or more. I don't even remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he told them to let me off. And there were two little towns. One had a 'stein' on the end, like 'Veringstein.' And one was 'berg,' 'Beringberg.' I don't remember what the name of those little towns were. Well, that guy took me to the wrong town. But I didn't know it. I'm standing and waiting. How long does it take to bring up the rear from two kilometers? Not that long, even though there was a whole echelon of people walking.

speaker: interviewee
text: Something was wrong. It was getting dusk. And I went into a house. And I started ordering around. I was so bad. I started ordering around this lady. And she was petrified of me. She was petrified. And I told her, I want some milk. And I want something to eat. And she said she didn't have it. And I say, yes, you do have it. Of course you have it. You just don't want to give it to me. And she did give it to me. She did have it. And she did give it to me. I say, if you don't give it to me, I'll tell the Russians on you. And she did give me. I was hungry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, to make a long story short, they never arrived there. And I was missing. And they were in the other town. And I started asking her about the other town. What is the closest thing to it? And she gave me the name. And I said, I bet you that's where I was supposed to go. And she says, how far is it? And she says, it's across that field.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was winter. And it was slushy and sloppy. And here I am with those boots, those wonderful boots. So I said, I don't care. I'm walking. So I got out. And I walked across that-- about a couple kilometers across the field. And the slush was this deep. And I got across there. And the Russians, they were so happy to see me because they were looking for me. My brother was absolutely devastated. He said, living through a war, and here, to lose her.

speaker: interviewer
text: He just-- what is he going to tell his mother? What is he going to tell mother about that I got lost in Germany after living through-- after being caught on the border and not being shot? He was devastated.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Russians treated me like I was princess. They put me up in the Burgermeister's house. I was completely undressed. And everything was dry-- was put on-- hung up to dry because I was wet. My shoes were put next to the-- to the-- to the fireplace to dry.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was put into this beautiful waterbed-- I mean, feather bed with, with warm bottles. That's how they used to warm the bed, with [GERMAN], you know? That's warm like those-- what do they call that in English? They're a form of bottles.

speaker: interviewee
text: They are made out of rubber. And they have hot water in them. And then they--

speaker: interviewer
text: Like thermoses?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Whatever. And they put it on. And it warms your tummy. And it warms the bed. And I was in this wonderful house. And they were all sleeping in a barn, the whole rest of the group. There must've been a couple hundred people. They're all sleeping in a barn. And I'm sleeping in the Burgermeister's house, in his best room with a-- anyhow. We had to go through some inspections. And, and then they asked-- the Russians asked us why we want to go across. And we told them because my mother is across. And I want to go and reunite with her. That's why. Couldn't say you want to be with the Americans. That would have been cutting your own throat. So I was briefed very well what to say. And the Russians were very nice to me. I had no problems at all. And we walked across. And I ended up-- at the end of December 1945, I ended up in Foehrenwald, Germany, which was a DP camp. It was already quite full by then. Amazing how many people reached to that place. And I was-- my brother deposited me, sort of-- like, literally deposited me with my uncle. And he survived by different means. He survived by hiding out. And my uncle and my cousin-- and he had since remarried already because his wife didn't make it through the war. He was already remarried to another woman. And they put me into that room with them. And he left me. And he went back to Poland to get Mother and my brother to bring them over. So this is how I ended up in Foehrenwald in a DP camp, which I stayed for six years. I got my education there, whatever it was-- not the whole six years, but at least four and a half. I learned Hebrew. The school was maintained on a-- on a Israeli-type from shlichim, which were reps from Israel, teachers who were teaching us. But actually, we had survivors themselves teach us who were professors from Poland, Hungary, from wherever they survived. They taught us. And we had a fantastic education in that short time. I actually went to school there four and a half years-- four years and something.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the time I got to the United States, I was 18 years old-- over 18. And I was-- I got to the United States by myself with my mother again. None of my brothers came. And that's, that's the hurt of the century, because they left way before. While we were in Foehrenwald in the DP camp, they all went their own way. And just my mother and I stayed in Foehrenwald all the years. My brother went to the Aliyah Hano'ar, which was the Youth Aliyah to Israel in 1947. My, my brother who survived by different means and came back to, to through France as a shaliah, which is a teacher-- to France. And he found out we are alive. And he came to Germany. But unfortunately, he contracted TB. And he was laid up for more than two years in a sanitarium. And after he got out, he went back to Israel. And my oldest brother, he never stayed in the camp. He stayed in Munich, which is a big town about 35 kilometers from the camp. So in the camp for the six years, I was just my mother and I.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was taken away on the second day of Rosh Hashanah in 1944. In Auschwitz, he was taken away. And this is when we, we commemorate his [YIDDISH], his memorial.

speaker: interviewer
text: I would like to look at some of the photographs of your experience in liberation. But first, tell me how you-- when you came to the United States and got married.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's like a whole world went through in between then. Since my brothers were not with me and my mother-- and we were staying on in Foehrenwald. And the camp was disintegrating. And there was practically very few people left. And we had to make a commitment to go somewhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I, one day, went and registered with the Jewish, Jewish Committee to go to immigrate to America. And they had their own quota. In other words, people that helped them to secure a quota somewhere in the United States. And my, my visa came very quickly, very quickly. I registered, and it seemed like in no time, I was called to go. So it was a very mixed bag. I didn't know whether I should go or shouldn't go. Here my brother ended up in Australia, my oldest brother. He went there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we couldn't immigrate there because they wouldn't take an older person-- my mother. And they wouldn't take younger people like me. They only wanted workers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I couldn't go to Australia. I couldn't go to Israel. And I couldn't go to anywhere else. So I decided I'm going to be a rebel. And I registered for America. And I ended up in the United States with the help of the Jewish Federation, I guess. And they had an opening in Detroit. And they sent us to Detroit.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in Detroit, we lived with a family. They gave us a room. The Jewish Federation gave us a room. I went to school for one semester. And then I had to go to work. And after a year and a half, I started supporting my mother and me. And when Mickey, my husband-- he was in Korea. When he came out of Korea, we met. And we got married in 1957 in Detroit, Michigan.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you've had two children?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we have two children. And their name is Linda and Danny. And Danny is named after Mickey's father and my father. And my daughter is named after my mother because Mickey's mother survived. And she was still alive when she was born. So. And we lived in Detroit for a very short time. And in 1958, we moved out to Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to look at some of the photographs.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's do that. May we cut? OK, first, your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Paula Lebovics. And the picture in front of you, I am that little girl in the front row. I was about six and a half or close to seven. This was approximately taken in Ostrowiec, Poland. And the date would be probably either 19-- late 1940, late 1940 or maybe beginning '41. This is my family. They are the Balters. And from left, you see my little brother, Josef-- Joska, as we called him. My mother's sitting in front of him. Then my oldest brother, Herschel, and my sister Chana and Haya and my father. You can see on the faces of Chana and my father, they had just recovered from typhus. This picture is a very important picture in Holocaust history because it was taken under duress. It was taken by the SS marching us to the photographers office due to the request of another brother, who is not on this picture, who was not in Poland at the time. And they took this picture and dressed us up. And as you can see, the flowers in front of me, and they made me put those hands on the flowers, which might have been artificial for all I know. But this was-- my two sisters are not alive on that picture. Neither is my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's cut.

speaker: interviewer
text: We are rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Paula Lebovics. And this is a picture in that famous walk in Birkenau when you come into Auschwitz. I am on that picture somewhere between those front children in the striped. My head is peeking out. Half my face is peeking out through the picture. This was taken by the Russians after liberation. And it was taken in 1945-- '45 in-- probably early February because they dressed us up. I don't think it was taken immediately after liberation. It was probably a few days gone. And they took a lot of movies and a lot of pictures that I recall at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Paula Lebovics. And I'm in Los Angeles. And this was also taken after liberation of Auschwitz. I think this photo appears almost everywhere. I am the person-- one-- let's see-- from the left, one, two, three, four. I'm, I'm the fifth one. I'm sort of stuck between two people there. And it was taken by the Russians. They dressed us in those striped suits. And they took these famous pictures. And they took movies.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is amazing, the way it was found. I went to Israel one time. And we walked through Yad Vashem. We've walked through many times. And I noticed on the way out, they had this in a very, very large form, almost taking up a half a wall. And I looked at this picture. And somehow my knees got weak. But I was not positive if it was me on that picture. I thought it was. And I waited till my brother caught up with me and to verify. And when he came by-- and he just grabbed his head. And he says, oh, my god. Oh, my god, this is you. And when the director of the museum at Yad Vashem-- we contacted him. He was overwhelmed because before that, no one has ever identified themselves through any of the pictures before. So he was overwhelmed. He did not know what to do with us. He took us into a screening room. And he showed us the photographs that I watched the Russians being taken after liberation of Auschwitz. And those invariably all ended up as evidence for the Nuremberg trials.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we are rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Paula Lebovics. And this is another picture from Auschwitz after liberation, taken by the Russians in approximately January or February of '45. I'm the third one from the front. You can see my little, swollen cheeks. I was 11 years old on this picture. And all of us-- most of us were pretty malnutritious at that point. But it took me a year and a half to get rid of my swollen cheeks. I recognize a lot of these people. Three of-- three of us, we keep in contact. The rest, I really don't know their whereabouts.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: What do I do?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us you name, and then this--

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Paula Lebovics. And this is-- this picture is my wedding picture. And the place was in Detroit, Michigan. And it was June 25, 1957 in Beth Aaron Synagogue. And my husband, Michael-- 'Mickey,' as we affectionately call him. This picture was at my wedding, which was unfortunately not a happy occasion, other than being a wedding, because my mother, who survived the war, died of-- succumbed to cancer of the pancreas three weeks before, before my marriage. So I, I did have two brothers that were there at the time. But I was still-- they still-- all by ourselves, in other words. I didn't have anybody. They just came in for the wedding. It's a time when I, I feel is very sad in my memory, not because of my-- besides that my mother had passed away, I felt alone and abandoned again, even though I was an adult and undertaking a road to, to build my own family. I did not feel I was at the right place. This is-- was not, for me, a good time to start a new life. But as the years went by, we, we learned to heal our wounds. And we raised two children. And this is 37 years later. And I'm in Los Angeles. And I guess my-- the next one will be the children's pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Paula Lebovics. And this was taken in Los Angeles, California. And the date is approximately 1961. And these are our two children. Mickey is my husband. And these are our two children. This is Linda. She's about three on that photo. And this is Danny, who is one. What else can you say?
